title: The Enchiridion (Handbook)
subtitle: Ἐγχειρίδιον Ἐπικτήτου — Manual of Stoic Ethics
author: Epictetus (recorded by Arrian)

recommendedTranslation.title: Enchiridion
recommendedTranslation.note: Most affordable standalone edition of the Enchiridion itself, with Long's clear Victorian-era translation.

alternativeTranslations.title: The Handbook (The Encheiridion)
alternativeTranslations.note: Concise, accurate modern translation widely used in university courses.

alternativeTranslations.title: Discourses, Fragments, Handbook
alternativeTranslations.note: Complete modern translation with the Discourses and fragments, scholarly introduction by Christopher Gill.

alternativeTranslations.title: Discourses and Selected Writings
alternativeTranslations.note: Lively modern translation with helpful introduction and notes.

alternativeTranslations.title: All the Works of Epictetus
alternativeTranslations.note: Historic eighteenth-century translation that popularized Epictetus in English.

excerpt: A compact manual of Stoic ethical advice compiled by Arrian from the lectures of Epictetus, a former slave turned philosopher. In 53 short chapters, the Enchiridion distills Stoicism to its practical essence: focus only on what is within your control, accept what is not, and align your will with nature. The most accessible entry point into ancient Stoic philosophy — a field manual for mental freedom that has shaped thinkers from Marcus Aurelius to modern cognitive therapists.
keyIdeas: Some things are up to us, others are not — the dichotomy of control is the master key to tranquillity
Events are neutral; our judgments about them cause suffering, not the events themselves
Freedom is not the absence of external constraint but mastery of one's own desires and aversions
We play roles on a stage — the wise person performs each role virtuously without attachment to outcomes
keyTakeaways: Distinguish sharply between what is within your control (judgments, choices, will) and what is not (body, reputation, wealth, other people's actions)
When frustrated or disturbed, examine your own judgment rather than blaming external events or other people
Practice negative visualization: before you embrace anything, consider its impermanence
Live your roles — as parent, child, citizen — with full commitment but without attachment to results
whoShouldRead: Anyone seeking a practical, no-nonsense introduction to Stoic philosophy
People struggling with anxiety, anger, or a sense of powerlessness over their circumstances
Readers who found Marcus Aurelius's Meditations inspiring but want the theoretical framework behind it
Students of philosophy looking for the most concise ancient statement of Stoic ethics
whoShouldSkip: Readers seeking systematic metaphysics or epistemology — Epictetus eschews theory for practice
Those uncomfortable with theistic framing (the Enchiridion assumes a providential universe governed by Zeus)
Anyone looking for modern psychological nuance on trauma or systemic injustice — the advice assumes radical individual responsibility
